name: Uniqueness of the Identity Element
statement: A binary operation has at most one identity element; if an identity exists, it is unique.
proof_sketch: Suppose e and f are both identity elements. Then e = e·f = f, because e is an identity gives e·f = f, and f is an identity gives e·f = e.

name: Inverse of Inverse and Product Inverse
name_norm: inverse of inverse and product inverse
proof_sketch: From uv = 1 follows v = u^{−1}. Thus x^{−1}x = 1 gives x = (x^{−1})^{−1}. For n=2, (xy)(y^{−1}x^{−1}) = 1, so y^{−1}x^{−1} = (xy)^{−1}; general case by induction.

name: Powers with Integer Exponents
statement: For a ∈ G: if n ≥ 0, a^n is the product of n copies of a (a^0 = 1, a^1 = a); if n < 0 with n = −m (m ≥ 0), then a^n = (a^m)^{−1}. In particular, a^{−1} is the inverse.
proof_sketch: None.

name: Consequences of Injectivity and Surjectivity for Homomorphisms
name_norm: consequences of injectivity and surjectivity for homomorphisms
statement: Let ϕ: A → B be a homomorphism of groups. If ϕ is injective, then A ≅ Im ϕ. If ϕ is surjective, then B ≅ A/Ker ϕ.
proof_sketch: If ϕ is injective, Ker ϕ = {1} and A ≅ A/Ker ϕ ≅ Im ϕ by the First Isomorphism Theorem. If ϕ is surjective, then B = Im ϕ and B ≅ A/Ker ϕ by the same theorem.

name: First Sylow Theorem
statement: Let G be a finite group and let p be a prime. If p^k divides |G|, then G has a subgroup of order p^k.
proof_sketch: First, for abelian G, existence of an element of order p is proved by induction on |G|: if |G|=p it is trivial; otherwise take a non-identity a. If p divides ord(a) we are done; else p divides |G|<a>|, so by induction G/<a> has an element of order p, and its preimage yields an element of order p in G. Then for general finite G, induction on |G|: if p divides |Z(G)|, take a subgroup A of order p in Z(G) (which is normal), then p^{k-1} divides |G/A|; by induction, G/A has a subgroup B/A of order p^{k-1}, so B has order p^k. If p does not divide |Z(G)|, use the class equation to find a conjugacy class C with |C| not divisible by p, so p^k divides the centralizer C(x) for x in C; by induction C(x) has a subgroup of order p^k.

name: Free product as a group
statement: If A ∩ B = 1, then the set A * B of all reduced nonempty words in A ∪ B is a group under the operation x . y = red(xy).
proof_sketch: Associativity follows as in Proposition 6.5. The one-letter word (1) is reduced and serves as identity. The inverse of a reduced word (x1,…,xn) is (xn^{-1},…,x1^{-1}), which is reduced and satisfies x x^{-1} → 1, x^{-1} x → 1.

name: Existence of Maximal Ideal Containing Any Proper Ideal
name_norm: existence of maximal ideal containing any proper ideal
statement: In a ring R with identity, every proper ideal is contained in a maximal ideal.
proof_sketch: Given a proper ideal I, let S be the set of all proper ideals of R that contain I, partially ordered by inclusion. Every chain in S has an upper bound in S, namely its union, which is proper (since no proper ideal contains the identity) and is an ideal by Proposition 2.5. S is nonempty because I ∈ S. By Zorn's lemma, S has a maximal element M, which is a maximal ideal containing I.

name: Conjugacy Action and Conjugacy Classes
statement: The action of G on itself by inner automorphisms is defined by g·x = gxg⁻¹ for all g, x ∈ G. The orbits under this action are the conjugacy classes of G; two elements are conjugate if they lie in the same conjugacy class.

name: Feit-Thompson Odd Order Theorem
statement: Every finite group of odd order is solvable (equivalently, all nonabelian finite simple groups have even order).
proof_sketch: Deep theorem proved by Feit and Thompson (1963); proof not provided in this text.

name: Factor Theorem
statement: Let R be commutative, r ∈ R, A ∈ R[X]. Then A is a multiple of X − r if and only if A(r) = 0.
proof_sketch: Polynomial division yields A = (X−r)B + S with deg S < 1, so S is constant. Evaluating at r gives S = A(r). Hence X−r divides A iff A(r)=0.

name: Decomposition into polynomial and polynomial-free part
name_norm: decomposition into polynomial and polynomial-free part
statement: Every rational fraction can be written uniquely as the sum of a polynomial and a polynomial-free fraction in reduced form.
proof_sketch: Existence: By Lemma 9.2 write the fraction in reduced form f/g. Polynomial division yields f = gq + r with deg r < deg g, giving f/g = q + r/g; r/g is polynomial-free and reduced. Uniqueness: Given two such decompositions, express both with a common reduced denominator and use the uniqueness from Lemma 9.2.

name: Definition of Coordinate Ring of an Algebraic Set
name_norm: definition of coordinate ring of an algebraic set
statement: The coordinate ring C(A) of an algebraic set A is the ring of all polynomial functions on A, with pointwise addition and multiplication. The field K is identified with the constant functions, making C(A) a ring extension of K generated by the coordinate functions.

name: Proposition 11.2: Maximal Ideals of the Coordinate Ring
name_norm: maximal ideals of the coordinate ring
statement: For an algebraic set A, let m_x = { f ∈ C(A) | f(x) = 0 } for x ∈ A. The map x ↦ m_x is a bijection from A onto the set of all maximal ideals of C(A).
proof_sketch: By 10.5, x ↦ I({x}) bijects K^n with maximal ideals of K[X1,...,Xn]. By 10.6, x ∈ A iff I(A) ⊆ I({x}), so it bijects A with maximal ideals containing I(A). These correspond to maximal ideals of K[X1,...,Xn]/I(A) ≅ C(A).

name: Preservation of Discreteness under Finite Extensions
name_norm: preservation of discreteness under finite extensions
statement: Let E be a finite extension of K, v a valuation on K, and w a valuation on E extending v. If v is discrete, then w is discrete.
proof_sketch: Using the injective homomorphism g ↦ g^e from G_w to G_v, discreteness of G_v (isomorphic to Z) implies G_w is also discrete.

name: Fundamental Equality for Complete Discrete Valuations
name_norm: fundamental equality for complete discrete valuations
statement: Let E be a finite extension of K, v a discrete nonarchimedean absolute value on K, and w a discrete nonarchimedean absolute value on E extending v. If K is complete with respect to v, then e(w:v) f(w:v) = [E:K].
proof_sketch: Existence and discreteness of w follow from 7.4 and 7.5. Choose generators v(p) = w(ρ)^e. Let f = f(w:v) and take β_j whose residues form a basis of F_w over F_v. Show the e f elements ρ^i β_j are linearly independent. Using completeness, expand any α ∈ E as Σ r_{ℓ i j} p^ℓ ρ^i β_j with r in a residue system, proving [E:K] = e f.

name: Height equals localization dimension
statement: The height of a prime ideal p of R equals the dimension of the localization Rp.
proof_sketch: Left to the reader; no proof provided.

name: Zero set of polynomials
statement: Let K be a field and K its algebraic closure. For a set S ⊆ K[X1, ..., Xn], the zero set is Z(S) = { x ∈ K^n | f(x) = 0 for all f ∈ S }.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Completeness of Grps and preservation by forgetful functor
name_norm: completeness of grps and preservation by forgetful functor
statement: The category Grps is complete; in fact, in Grps, a limit can be assigned to every diagram. Moreover, the forgetful functor from Grps to Sets preserves all limits.
proof_sketch: Given a diagram D in Grps over a graph G, form the standard limit L in Sets as the set of families (x_i) in the product of all D_i satisfying D_a(x_i)=x_j for edges a:i→j. Since each D_a is a homomorphism, L is a subgroup of the product, and the projections λ_i are homomorphisms. Any cone ϕ:A→D gives a unique cone map to L that is a homomorphism, so L is the limit in Grps. The forgetful functor preserves this limit because the underlying set and cone coincide with the Set-limit.

name: Hom functor preserves limits
statement: Let C be a locally small category. For every object A of C, the functor Hom_C(A, −) preserves all existing limits. In fact, λ is a limit cone of a diagram D in C if and only if Hom_C(A, λ) is a limit cone of the diagram Hom_C(A, D) for every object A of C. Dually, Hom_C(−, A) sends colimit cones to limit cones and colimits to limits.
proof_sketch: Exercise; uses that Hom functors preserve products and equalizers, hence all limits, and the characterization via the Yoneda embedding.

name: Finitely Generated Ideals Characterization of Noetherian Rings
name_norm: finitely generated ideals characterization of noetherian rings
statement: A commutative ring R is Noetherian if and only if every ideal of R is finitely generated.
proof_sketch: (⇒) Let a be an ideal. The set S of all finitely generated ideals contained in a has a maximal element s by a.c.c. For any a∈a, s+(a) is finitely generated, lies in S, so s = s+(a), hence a∈s and a=s is finitely generated. (⇐) Suppose every ideal is finitely generated. Given a1⊆a2⊆···, their union a is an ideal, generated by some a1,...,ak. Each ai ∈ a_{n_i}; let N = max n_i. Then a ⊆ aN, so the chain stabilizes at N.

name: Existence and Uniqueness of Primitive Representation
name_norm: existence and uniqueness of primitive representation
statement: Let o be a valuation ring of a field K and let m be its maximal ideal. Every nonzero polynomial f ∈ K[X] can be written in the form f(X) = t f*(X), where t ∈ K, t ≠ 0, and f*(X) ∈ o[X] is primitive; moreover, t and f* are unique up to multiplication by units of o.
proof_sketch: Take a valuation v with valuation ring o. For f = Σ a_i X^i, define V(f) = max_i v(a_i). Since f ≠ 0, V(f) = v(a_t) for some t, and then f = a_t f* where f* ∈ o[X] has a coefficient not in m, hence primitive. Uniqueness: if a g = b h with g, h primitive, then v(a) = V(a g) = V(b h) = v(b), so a/b is a unit and h = (a/b)g.

name: Gauss's Lemma for Valuation Rings
statement: Let o be a valuation ring of a field K. If f and g ∈ o[X] are primitive, then f g is primitive.
proof_sketch: Let f = Σ a_i X^i, g = Σ b_j X^j. Take a valuation v such that o is its valuation ring. Choose the smallest indices i, j such that v(a_i) and v(b_j) are minimal. The coefficient of X^{i+j} in fg then has minimal valuation and is not in the maximal ideal m, so fg is primitive.

name: Algebraic variety definition
statement: An algebraic set A ⊆ K^n is irreducible, or an algebraic variety, if A ≠ Ø and A is the zero set of a prime ideal.

name: Irreducible decomposition of algebraic sets
statement: Every algebraic set in K^n is uniquely an irredundant finite union of algebraic varieties.
proof_sketch: A semiprime ideal is a finite irredundant intersection of unique prime ideals (primary decomposition + radical). By the correspondence in Corollary 10.6, the algebraic set is the union of the zero sets of those primes, which are algebraic varieties.

name: Subgroups of Cyclic Groups are Cyclic
name_norm: subgroups of cyclic groups are cyclic
statement: Every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic.
proof_sketch: Let G = ⟨g⟩ and H ≤ G. If H = {1}, it is cyclic. Otherwise, let n be the smallest positive integer with gⁿ ∈ H. Then H = ⟨gⁿ⟩, because for any gᵐ ∈ H, write m = qn + r with 0 ≤ r < n; gʳ ∈ H forces r = 0.

name: Cyclic Group of Roots of Unity and Primitive Roots
name_norm: cyclic group of roots of unity and primitive roots
statement: The complex nth roots of unity form a cyclic group. ω_k = cos(2πk/n) + i sin(2πk/n) generates this group if and only if gcd(k, n) = 1 (i.e., ω_k is a primitive nth root of unity).
proof_sketch: All nth roots are powers of ω_1, so ⟨ω_1⟩ is cyclic of order n. An element ω_1ᵏ has order n/gcd(k,n), so it generates the group iff gcd(k,n) = 1.

name: a-adic Filtration
statement: For an ideal a of a commutative ring R, the a-adic filtration is the filtration a ⊇ a^2 ⊇ · · · ⊇ a^i ⊇ a^{i+1} ⊇ · · ·, where a^n is the ideal generated by all products a1···an with ai ∈ a.

name: Completion relative to a filtration
statement: Let A: a1 ⊇ a2 ⊇ · · · be a filtration on a commutative ring R. The completion of R relative to A is the ring R̂_A of all infinite sequences (x1 + a1, …, xi + ai, …) with xi ∈ R and xi + ai = xj + ai whenever j ≥ i (equivalently xi+1 ∈ xi + ai for all i ≥ 1). R̂_A is a subring of ∏ R/ai.

name: Transcendence degree of an algebra
statement: Let A be an algebra over a field K. The transcendence degree of A is defined as trdeg(A) := sup { |T| | T ⊆ A is finite and algebraically independent }, with trdeg(A) ∈ ℕ₀ ∪ {-1, ∞}, where -1 occurs if A = {0} is the zero ring (sup ∅ := -1).

name: R-Algebra Homomorphism
statement: An R-algebra homomorphism between R-algebras A (α: R→A) and B (β: R→B) is a ring homomorphism ϕ: A → B such that ϕ ∘ α = β.
proof_sketch: None

name: Automorphism Existence for Prime Ideals in Integral Closure
name_norm: automorphism existence for prime ideals in integral closure
statement: Let N be a field and K ⊆ N a subfield such that N is finite and normal over K. Let R ⊆ K be a subring that is integrally closed in K, and let S ⊆ N be the integral closure of R in N. Then for two prime ideals Q, ̃Q ∈ Spec(S) with R ∩ Q = R ∩ ̃Q, there exists σ ∈ G ≔ Aut_K(N) with ̃Q = σ(Q).
proof_sketch: Take a ∈ ̃Q. The product over σ ∈ G of σ(a) lies in N^G, so Lemma 8.15 yields an n with b = ∏ σ(a)^{p^n} ∈ K, where p = char(K). As all σ(a) are integral over R, b is integral over R and actually lies in R. Moreover, b ∈ R ∩ ̃Q = R ∩ Q ⊆ Q. Since Q is prime, some σ(a) ∈ Q. Thus ̃Q is contained in the union of the σ(Q). By prime avoidance (Lemma 7.7), ̃Q ⊆ σ(Q) for some σ. Because R ∩ σ(Q) = R ∩ Q = R ∩ ̃Q, Theorem 8.12(b) forces equality ̃Q = σ(Q).

name: Definition: Lying over and going up
name_norm: definition: lying over and going up
statement: For an integral extension R ⊆ S, a prime ideal Q ∈ Spec(S) is said to lie over a prime ideal P ∈ Spec(R) if R ∩ Q = P. If additionally an ideal I ⊆ S is contained in Q, we say that we are going up from I.
proof_sketch: None (definition)

name: Locally Principal Ideal
statement: A fractional ideal I of an integral domain R is called locally principal if for every prime ideal P ∈ Spec(R) the localization IP is a principal ideal of the local ring RP, i.e., IP = (a)RP for some a ∈ I.
proof_sketch: None (definition introduced in Proposition 14.6(c)).

name: Chains of prime ideals in an affine algebra
name_norm: chains of prime ideals in an affine algebra
statement: Let A be an affine algebra and let P0 ⊊ P1 ⊊ ... ⊊ Pn be a maximal chain of prime ideals Pi ∈ Spec(A). Then n = dim(A/P0). In particular, if A is equidimensional (which is always the case if A is an affine domain), then every maximal chain of prime ideals of A has length equal to dim(A).
proof_sketch: Induction on n. Replace A by A/P0 to reduce to the case where A is an affine domain and P0 = {0}. If n = 0, then {0} is a maximal ideal, so A is a field and dim(A) = 0 = n. The inductive step continues by considering the next prime ideal, but the remainder of the proof is not included in the excerpt.

name: Initial form ideal
statement: For an ideal I ⊆ K[x1,…,xn], the initial form ideal is Iin := (fin | f ∈ I \ {0}) in K[x1,…,xn].

name: Properties of Covering Projections
statement: Let p: Y → X be a covering projection. Then (i) For every x ∈ X, p^{-1}(x) is a discrete subset of Y. (ii) p is a local homeomorphism. (iii) The topology on X is the quotient topology it inherits from Y via the map p.

name: Unique Path Lifting
statement: Let p: Y → X be a covering projection. Let x_0 ∈ X be arbitrary and let y_0 ∈ Y be any point with p(y_0) = x_0. Let f: I → X be an arbitrary map with f(0) = x_0. Then f has a unique lifting f̃: I → Y with f̃(0) = y_0, i.e., there is a unique f̃: I → Y with f̃(0) = y_0 making p ∘ f̃ = f.

name: Bilinear form definition
statement: Let R be a commutative ring and M a free R-module. A bilinear form ⟨ , ⟩ : M × M → R is a function that is linear in both arguments: (1) ⟨r₁m₁ + r₂m₂, m⟩ = r₁⟨m₁,m⟩ + r₂⟨m₂,m⟩, (2) ⟨m, r₁m₁ + r₂m₂⟩ = r₁⟨m,m₁⟩ + r₂⟨m,m₂⟩ for all r₁,r₂ ∈ R and m₁,m₂,m ∈ M.
proof_sketch: None (definition)

name: Interior and Boundary of Manifold with Boundary
name_norm: interior and boundary of manifold with boundary
statement: If M is an n-manifold with nonempty boundary, then int(M) is an n-manifold and ∂M is an (n-1)-manifold.
proof_sketch: The first statement is clear. For the second, take x in ∂M and a homeomorphism ϕx: Rn+ → U with x ∈ ϕx(∂Rn+). Restricting ϕx to ∂Rn+ (which is homeomorphic to Rn-1) gives a homeomorphism onto U ∩ ∂M.

name: Cover Characterization of Manifold with Boundary
name_norm: cover characterization of manifold with boundary
statement: M is an n-manifold with boundary if and only if M is a Hausdorff space and M has an open cover {Uα} with each Uα homeomorphic to either Rn or Rn+.
proof_sketch: Analogous to Lemma 6.1.2.
